prophecies.name: The Lord, the King of Israel, in the Midst of Zion
prophecies.date: Written c. 640–625 BC during the reign of King Josiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The prophetic expectation of the Lord Himself dwelling among His people as the 'King of Israel' is fulfilled in the Incarnation and explicitly ascribed to Jesus during His ministry and Triumphal Entry.

prophecies.name: The One Who Ascends and Descends from Heaven
prophecies.date: Composed mainly c. 950–700 BC during the reigns of Solomon and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus alludes to the rhetorical question of Proverbs 30:4, identifying Himself as the Son of Man who uniquely descended from heaven.

prophecies.name: The Servant Known as The Branch
prophecies.date: Written c. 520–480 BC during the post-exilic restoration

nt_fulfillments.note: The Greek word used for 'Branch' in the LXX is 'Anatole', which translates to 'Dayspring' or 'Sunrise' as applied to Christ's incarnation in Luke 1:78.

prophecies.name: The Meek King Riding on a Donkey
prophecies.date: Written c. 520–480 BC during the post-exilic restoration

prophecies.name: Betrayal for Thirty Pieces of Silver
prophecies.date: Written c. 520–480 BC during the post-exilic restoration

nt_fulfillments.note: Matthew explicitly notes the fulfillment of the thirty pieces of silver being cast into the temple and used for the potter's field. The LXX translates the Hebrew word for 'potter' as 'furnace'.

prophecies.name: Looking Upon the Pierced One
prophecies.date: Written c. 520–480 BC during the post-exilic restoration

nt_fulfillments.note: While this specific LXX manuscript reads 'because they have mocked', the Gospel of John cites this exact verse translating the underlying Hebrew as 'pierced'.

prophecies.name: Striking the Shepherd and Scattering the Sheep
prophecies.date: Written c. 520–480 BC during the post-exilic restoration

prophecies.name: The Messiah's Birth in Bethlehem and Eternal Origins
prophecies.date: Written c. 735–700 BC during the reigns of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Matthew explicitly cites this prophecy when the chief priests and scribes are asked where the Christ is to be born. Furthermore, the LXX translation emphasizing 'his goings forth were from the beginning, even from eternity' was a pivotal proof-text for the Early Church Fathers in defending the eternal pre-existence and uncreated nature of the Son of God.

prophecies.name: Familial Division Caused by the Messiah's Coming
prophecies.date: Written c. 735–700 BC during the reigns of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus directly applies this prophetic description of societal and familial breakdown to the eschatological division caused by His own earthly ministry and the cost of discipleship.

prophecies.name: The Seed of the Woman (Protoevangelium)
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Recognized historically as the first messianic prophecy. The Epistles explicitly identify Jesus as the 'seed' born of a woman who destroys the devil's work and crushes Satan, despite the bruising of His heel on the cross.

prophecies.name: All Nations Blessed Through Abraham's Seed
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: While Matthew establishes the lineage, Paul explicitly identifies Christ as the singular 'seed' of Abraham through whom the prophetic blessing of the Gentiles is fulfilled.

prophecies.name: Angels Commanded to Worship the Son
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: The author of Hebrews cites this verse (or the closely parallel Deuteronomy 32:43 in the LXX) to demonstrate the Son's divine superiority, applying the command for angels to worship God directly to the bringing of the Firstborn into the world.

prophecies.name: The Son as the Eternal Creator
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Hebrews explicitly quotes this Psalm and addresses it to the Son, identifying Jesus as the eternal Lord who laid the foundation of the earth and will remain unchangeable.

prophecies.name: Ascension and Giving of Gifts
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Cited by Paul as foreshadowing Christ's ascension and the subsequent outpouring of spiritual gifts to the Church.

prophecies.name: Hated Without a Cause
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: Zeal for God's House and Bearing Reproaches
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul applies the second half of the verse to Christ's self-sacrificial nature and his bearing of reproaches meant for God.

prophecies.name: Given Gall and Vinegar
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: Desolation of the Betrayer
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Peter explicitly cites this verse regarding the fate and replacement of Judas Iscariot.

prophecies.name: Kings Bring Gifts and Worship
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Universally recognized by the Early Church Fathers as a typological prophecy fulfilled by the Magi (often traditioned as kings) bringing gifts to the infant Christ.

prophecies.name: The Davidic Covenant: The Son of God and the Everlasting Throne
prophecies.date: Written c. 960–930 BC after the division of the kingdom, compiling earlier prophetic records

nt_fulfillments.note: The angel Gabriel explicitly applies the promise of an everlasting throne from the Davidic Covenant to Jesus.
The author of Hebrews directly quotes 2 Samuel 7:14 ('I will be to him a father, and he shall be to me a son') to establish the divine sonship of Jesus.
Peter identifies this promise in his Pentecost sermon, declaring that God swore to David that He would raise up the Christ to sit on his throne.

prophecies.name: All Nations Blessed in the Seed of Abraham
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: While Matthew establishes the lineage, Paul in Galatians provides the explicit theological explanation that the singular 'seed' of Abraham promised to bless the nations is directly Christ.

prophecies.name: The Promised Seed Called Through Isaac
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The Epistles explicitly interpret this verse to explain the theology of the children of the promise versus children of the flesh.

prophecies.name: God Will Provide Himself a Lamb for the Sacrifice
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The Early Church Fathers extensively cited the binding of Isaac (Akedah) and Abraham's prophetic statement that God would provide His own sheep as a clear typological prophecy of Jesus, the Lamb of God.

prophecies.name: All Nations Blessed in the Seed of Isaac
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

prophecies.name: The Son of Man as the Ladder to Heaven
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus explicitly appropriates the imagery of Jacob's ladder, identifying Himself as the direct mediator between heaven and earth upon whom the angels ascend and descend.

prophecies.name: All Tribes Blessed in the Seed of Jacob
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

prophecies.name: The Anointed Lord Captured
prophecies.date: Written c. 586 BC immediately following the destruction of Jerusalem

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers, including Justin Martyr and Irenaeus, explicitly cited the 'anointed Lord' (Christos Kuriou) being taken in their snares as a prophecy of the capture and crucifixion of Jesus Christ.

prophecies.name: Struck on the Cheek and Reproached
prophecies.date: Written c. 586 BC immediately following the destruction of Jerusalem

prophecies.name: Given Gall in Suffering
prophecies.date: Written c. 586 BC immediately following the destruction of Jerusalem

prophecies.name: Conspiracy to Bind the Just One
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The LXX uniquely translates this verse as 'Let us bind the just, for he is burdensome to us', which was frequently cited by Early Church Fathers (like Justin Martyr) as a prophetic foreshadowing of the Sanhedrin binding Jesus and delivering Him to Pilate.

prophecies.name: Isaiah's Vision of the Lord's Glory and the People's Unbelief
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: John explicitly states that Isaiah saw Jesus' glory when he beheld the Lord of Hosts on the throne. Jesus also directly quotes the prophecy regarding the people's hardened hearts to explain His use of parables.

prophecies.name: A Virgin Shall Conceive Emmanuel
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Matthew cites the LXX translation of this verse ('parthenos' meaning virgin) to demonstrate that Jesus is the prophesied Emmanuel born of a virgin.

prophecies.name: A Stone of Stumbling and Rock of Offense
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul and Peter both explicitly cite this verse, identifying Christ as the prophesied sanctuary who is also a stumbling stone to the disobedient.

prophecies.name: A Great Light in Galilee of the Gentiles
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Matthew quotes this prophecy to explain Jesus beginning His public ministry in Capernaum, located in the region of Zebulun and Naphtali.

prophecies.name: A Child Born as the Messenger of Great Counsel
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The LXX uniquely translates the child's title as 'Messenger of great counsel' (Angel of Great Counsel), which the Early Church Fathers heavily cited regarding the pre-existent Son revealing the Father's will. The Angel Gabriel's annunciation clearly echoes the promise of His eternal dominion on David's throne.

prophecies.name: The Rod of Jesse and the Resting of the Spirit
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The prophecy of the Spirit of God resting upon the shoot of Jesse is visibly fulfilled at Christ's baptism, where the Holy Spirit descends and remains on Him.

prophecies.name: The Root of Jesse as the Hope of the Gentiles
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul directly quotes the LXX translation of this verse to demonstrate that Jesus is the prophesied ruler over the Gentiles in whom they will hope.

prophecies.name: Israel Will Seek David Their King
prophecies.date: Written c. 755–715 BC during the final decades of the northern kingdom of Israel

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers widely understood 'David their king' as a direct reference to the Messiah, fulfilled in Jesus Christ, the eternal King from the lineage of David.

prophecies.name: Raised on the Third Day
prophecies.date: Written c. 755–715 BC during the final decades of the northern kingdom of Israel

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul's statement that Christ was raised on the third day 'according to the Scriptures' is recognized by Patristic writers as a typological fulfillment of Hosea 6:3.

prophecies.name: The Son Called Out of Egypt
prophecies.date: Written c. 755–715 BC during the final decades of the northern kingdom of Israel

nt_fulfillments.note: While the LXX translates the Hebrew as 'his children', Matthew directly cites the Hebrew tradition ('my son') to show typological fulfillment in the Holy Family's return from Egypt.

prophecies.name: The Defeat of Death and Hades
prophecies.date: Written c. 755–715 BC during the final decades of the northern kingdom of Israel

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul explicitly quotes this verse to declare Christ's absolute victory over death and the grave through His resurrection.

prophecies.name: The Plot Against the Righteous Son of God
prophecies.date: Written c. 1st century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Wisdom 2:18 is explicitly echoed in the mocking of Jesus by the religious leaders at the cross.
The Righteous One's boast that God is His Father aligns with the controversies leading to the passion in John's Gospel.

prophecies.name: Wisdom as the Brightness of Everlasting Light
prophecies.date: Written c. 1st century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The author of Hebrews applies the exact Greek terminology (apaugasma) from this verse to describe the eternal Son, establishing Wisdom as a Christological type.

prophecies.name: An Eternal Successor on the Throne of David
prophecies.date: Written c. 560–550 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: These verses reiterate the Davidic Covenant, promising that a descendant of David will continually sit on the throne of Israel. While historically applied to Solomon's line conditionally, the New Testament universally identifies Jesus Christ as the ultimate and eternal fulfillment of this Davidic promise.

prophecies.name: The Preserved Remnant and Root of David
prophecies.date: Written c. 180–175 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The promise that God would not abolish the posterity of his elect but give 'a root to David' points directly to the Messianic continuation of the Davidic line, culminating in Jesus Christ, who is explicitly called the Root of David.

prophecies.name: Elijah the Forerunner Turning Hearts
prophecies.date: Written c. 180–175 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The Gospel of Luke applies this specific prophetic expectation of turning the hearts of the fathers to the children directly to John the Baptist, who prepared the way for the Messiah in the spirit and power of Elijah.

prophecies.name: The Lord, the Father of My Lord
prophecies.date: Written c. 180–175 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: This verse was utilized heavily by Early Church Fathers, such as Cyprian of Carthage and Athanasius, as explicit Old Testament evidence for the distinction of Persons between God the Father and God the Son (the Lord).

prophecies.name: The Tender Branch Planted on a High Mountain
prophecies.date: Written c. 593–571 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus deliberately draws upon this prophetic imagery in the Parable of the Mustard Seed, describing the Messianic Kingdom as a small shoot that becomes a great tree where the birds of the air (the Gentile nations) find shelter.

prophecies.name: The Crown Reserved for the Rightful King
prophecies.date: Written c. 593–571 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: The phrase 'until he comes to whom it belongs' parallels the Shiloh prophecy in Genesis 49:10. It anticipates the removal of the earthly Davidic crown until the arrival of the Messiah, which is fulfilled when Gabriel announces that Jesus will receive the eternal throne of His father David.

prophecies.name: The Pre-Incarnate Vision of the Son of Man on the Throne
prophecies.date: Written c. 593–571 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers interpreted the vision of the likeness of a man upon the heavenly throne as a Christophany, a typological prophecy of the Divine Word who would take on human flesh.

prophecies.name: The Title 'Son of Man'
prophecies.date: Written c. 593–571 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: Ezekiel is repeatedly addressed as 'Son of man,' establishing a typological framework for the representative human prophet. Jesus adopts this as His primary self-designation.

prophecies.name: The Prophet Bears the Iniquity of the People
prophecies.date: Written c. 593–571 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: Ezekiel typologically prefigures the Messiah by being commanded to physically bear the iniquities of Israel and Judah, foreshadowing the Suffering Servant who bears the sins of the world.

prophecies.name: The Promise of a New Heart and Spirit
prophecies.date: Written c. 593–571 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: A direct prophecy of the New Covenant inaugurated by the Messiah, where God replaces the heart of stone with a heart of flesh, fulfilled through Christ's redemptive work and the giving of the Spirit.

prophecies.name: The Anointing of the Chosen Shepherd-King from Bethlehem
prophecies.date: Written c. 960–930 BC after the division of the kingdom, compiling earlier prophetic records

nt_fulfillments.note: David's selection from Bethlehem and his physical anointing, where the Spirit of the Lord came upon him, serves as the foundational typological prophecy of the Messiah ('Anointed One'). Early Church Fathers universally recognized this as prefiguring Christ's baptism by the Spirit and His eternal Davidic kingship.

prophecies.name: Walking on the Sea as on Firm Ground
prophecies.date: Written c. 6th century BC, though depicting events from the patriarchal era c. 2000–1800 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers frequently cited Job 9:8 as a typological prophecy of Christ's earthly ministry, demonstrating His divinity by performing an action explicitly and exclusively attributed to God in the Old Testament.

prophecies.name: Elijah Sent to the Gentile Widow in Zarephath
prophecies.date: Written c. 560–550 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus explicitly cites Elijah being sent to the widow of Zarephath in Sidon as a typological justification for His own rejection by Israel and the extension of His Messianic grace and ministry to the Gentiles.

prophecies.name: Elijah's Forty-Day Fast in the Wilderness
prophecies.date: Written c. 560–550 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: Elijah's forty-day and forty-night fast in the wilderness while journeying to Mount Horeb was universally understood by the Early Church Fathers as a typological foreshadowing of Christ's forty-day fast in the wilderness prior to His public ministry.

prophecies.name: The Lord Appearing in the Gentle Breeze
prophecies.date: Written c. 560–550 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers, such as Irenaeus, interpreted the Lord not appearing in the destructive wind, earthquake, or fire, but rather in the 'voice of a gentle breeze', as a typological prefiguration of the Word's humble and gentle Incarnation, contrasting the terrors of the Old Covenant with the grace of the New.

prophecies.name: The Coming of the Messiah
prophecies.date: Written c. 612–599 BC prior to the Babylonian invasion of Judah

nt_fulfillments.note: The LXX translation of Habakkuk 2:3 personalizes the prophecy by translating 'it' (the vision) as 'he' ('he will surely come'), which the author of Hebrews explicitly applies to the coming Christ.

prophecies.name: Revealed Between Two Living Creatures
prophecies.date: Written c. 612–599 BC prior to the Babylonian invasion of Judah

nt_fulfillments.note: The unique LXX reading 'known between the two living creatures' (ἐν μέσῳ δύο ζώων γνωσθήσῃ) was universally cited by Early Church Fathers, such as Origen and Augustine, as a prophecy of Christ's nativity in the manger, often typified by the ox and the ass.

prophecies.name: The Ascension of the Lord
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers universally interpreted this verse as a direct prophecy of Christ's Ascension into heaven to the right hand of the Father.

prophecies.name: Betrayal by a Familiar Friend
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: This lament of David is read as explicit typology by the Early Church, pointing to Judas Iscariot who shared fellowship and the Last Supper with Christ before betraying Him.

prophecies.name: The Eternal Reign of the Messianic King
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: The Eternal Throne of the House of David
prophecies.date: Written c. 450–425 BC during the post-exilic restoration

nt_fulfillments.note: God's reiteration of the Davidic covenant to Solomon and Abijah—promising an everlasting kingdom and a descendant who will never fail to rule—is fulfilled in the eternal Kingship of Jesus Christ.

prophecies.name: God Will Appear on Earth and Dwell Among Men
prophecies.date: Written c. 2nd–1st century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Baruch 3:35-37 was one of the most widely and universally cited texts by the Early Church Fathers (including Irenaeus, Cyprian, Augustine, and John of Damascus) as a definitive prophecy of the Incarnation, describing God Himself appearing on earth and conversing with humanity.

prophecies.name: The Preparation of the Way for God's Glory
prophecies.date: Written c. 2nd–1st century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Echoing Isaiah 40:4, Baruch envisions the leveling of mountains and valleys to prepare the way for Israel to walk in the 'glory of God.' The Gospels explicitly cite this prophetic imagery as fulfilled by John the Baptist preparing the way for Jesus.

prophecies.name: The One Shepherd, My Servant David
prophecies.date: Written c. 593–571 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus explicitly identifies Himself as the Good Shepherd, fulfilling the promise of a single shepherd from the line of David who will personally tend God's flock.

prophecies.name: David as Prince and Shepherd Forever
prophecies.date: Written c. 593–571 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: The angel Gabriel declares to Mary that Jesus will be given the throne of His father David and will reign as the eternal prince over the house of Jacob.

prophecies.name: Wisdom Created Before All Things
prophecies.date: Written c. 180–175 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Parallel to Proverbs 8:22, early Church Fathers (such as Athanasius) explicitly cited these verses regarding the personified Wisdom to articulate the eternal generation and incarnation of the Word (Logos).

prophecies.name: The Pre-existent Wisdom Proceeding from the Father
prophecies.date: Written c. 180–175 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers extensively cited the personified Wisdom of Sirach 24, who 'came out of the mouth of the most High' and was established 'before the world', as a typological revelation of Christ, the eternal Logos.

prophecies.name: Wisdom Tabernacling Among Men
prophecies.date: Written c. 180–175 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Wisdom being commanded to 'let your dwelling be in Jacob' (literally translated as 'pitch your tent' or 'tabernacle') was seen by Patristic writers as prefiguring the Word becoming flesh and tabernacling among humanity.

prophecies.name: The Bread of Life and Living Water
prophecies.date: Written c. 180–175 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus draws upon the Wisdom motif of eating and drinking in Sirach 24, presenting Himself as the ultimate fulfillment who permanently satisfies spiritual hunger and thirst.

prophecies.name: The Outpouring of the Holy Spirit and Salvation in the Lord's Name
prophecies.date: Written c. 835–796 BC (early pre-exilic dating) or c. 500–400 BC (post-exilic dating)

nt_fulfillments.note: Peter quotes this entire passage at Pentecost, confirming that the pouring out of the Spirit is the direct result of the exalted Christ's ascension.
Jesus draws on the cosmic signs of the sun and moon being darkened to describe the events surrounding the coming of the Son of Man.
Paul explicitly applies 'calling on the name of the Lord' from Joel 2:32 to faith in Jesus Christ, utilizing it to demonstrate Christ's divine identity.

prophecies.name: The Righteous King on the Throne of David
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Establishes the Messiah's eternal, righteous rule on the throne of David.

prophecies.name: The Lord's Descent into Egypt
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers explicitly cited this as fulfilled during the Holy Family's flight to Egypt, traditionally associated with the falling of Egyptian idols at Christ's arrival.

prophecies.name: The Bearer of the Key of David
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Originally spoken concerning Eliakim, this verse is explicitly applied to Jesus Christ in Revelation as the one holding absolute, unchallengeable authority over God's kingdom.

prophecies.name: The Victor Over Death
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The New Testament explicitly quotes this verse to declare the ultimate defeat of death through Christ's final victory.

prophecies.name: The Precious Cornerstone in Zion
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The Apostles Peter and Paul both explicitly identify Jesus Christ as this precious, chosen cornerstone laid in Zion by God.

prophecies.name: The Messiah Healing the Deaf and Blind
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus directly alludes to this prophecy to prove to John the Baptist's disciples that He is indeed the expected Messiah performing the prophesied signs.

prophecies.name: The Bronze Serpent as a Sign of Salvation
prophecies.date: Written c. 1st century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Wisdom explicitly interprets the bronze serpent event as a 'sign of salvation' saving those who turn to the Saviour of all, which Jesus cites as a typological prophecy of His crucifixion.

prophecies.name: The Bread from Heaven
prophecies.date: Written c. 1st century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The 'angels' food' and 'bread from heaven' are used typologically by Christ to declare Himself as the true Bread of Life sustained by the Word of God.

prophecies.name: The Almighty Word Leaps Down from Heaven
prophecies.date: Written c. 1st century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers prominently cited this passage in reference to the Incarnation, viewing the Word of judgment descending upon Egypt as a typological prophecy of the Divine Word (Logos) descending into the world to assume flesh.

prophecies.name: The Eternal Deliverer and the Resurrection of the Flesh
prophecies.date: Written c. 6th century BC, though depicting events from the patriarchal era c. 2000–1800 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Job's declaration of an eternal Deliverer who will raise up his flesh from the earth was universally cited by the Early Church Fathers (such as Clement of Rome in 1 Clement 26) as a direct prophecy of Christ the Redeemer and the future bodily resurrection.

prophecies.name: The Righteous Sufferer is Mocked and Spat Upon
prophecies.date: Written c. 6th century BC, though depicting events from the patriarchal era c. 2000–1800 BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Job's innocent suffering, specifically being made a byword and having mockers spit in his face, was extensively interpreted by Patristic authors (such as Gregory the Great) as a clear typology of Christ's humiliations during the Passion.

prophecies.name: The Sign of Jonah: Three Days and Three Nights
prophecies.date: Written c. 785–760 BC during the reign of Jeroboam II

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus explicitly establishes Jonah's three days and three nights in the belly of the sea creature as the definitive typological sign of His own burial in the 'heart of the earth' and subsequent resurrection.

prophecies.name: Deliverance from the Belly of Sheol
prophecies.date: Written c. 785–760 BC during the reign of Jeroboam II

nt_fulfillments.note: Jonah's cry from the 'belly of hell' (Sheol) and his life being restored from the depths was read by Early Church Fathers as a prophetic type of Christ's descent into Hades and His victory over death.

prophecies.name: The Preaching to the Gentiles and Their Repentance
prophecies.date: Written c. 785–760 BC during the reign of Jeroboam II

nt_fulfillments.note: Christ explicitly parallels His ministry with Jonah's, declaring Himself 'greater than Jonah' and using Nineveh's repentance as a typological foreshadowing of the Gentiles receiving His message.

prophecies.name: The Anointed One Preaches Glad Tidings
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus reads this exact passage in the synagogue at Nazareth and explicitly declares its fulfillment in Himself.

prophecies.name: Proclamation of the Coming Savior to Zion
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Matthew combines the opening phrase of Isaiah 62:11 ('Say to the daughter of Sion') with Zechariah 9:9 to describe the Triumphal Entry of Christ.

prophecies.name: Treading the Winepress of God's Wrath
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Revelation explicitly draws upon this imagery to describe the victorious, returning Christ wearing a robe dipped in blood and treading the winepress of the wrath of God.

prophecies.name: Revealed to the Gentiles and Hands Stretched Out
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul quotes these verses to explain the inclusion of the Gentiles and Israel's rejection. Furthermore, early Church Fathers (such as Justin Martyr) universally cited 'stretched forth my hands' as a typological prophecy of Christ's posture on the cross.

prophecies.name: The Miraculous Birth of the Male Child
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Echoed in Revelation's vision of the woman bringing forth the male child, this verse was prominently cited by early Church Fathers like Irenaeus as a prophecy predicting the miraculous, painless Virgin Birth of Christ.

prophecies.name: The Messianic Ruler from the Tribe of Judah
prophecies.date: Written c. 450–425 BC during the post-exilic restoration

nt_fulfillments.note: The author of Hebrews affirms this Old Testament expectation, stating clearly that the Lord sprang from the tribe of Judah.
Christ is explicitly given the messianic title 'Lion of the tribe of Judah', the victorious ruler.

prophecies.note: While 1 Chronicles 1-15 is predominantly a historical and genealogical record, 1 Chronicles 5:2 explicitly reiterates the prophetic promise (originating in Genesis 49:10) that the supreme Ruler would emerge from the tribe of Judah, establishing the required lineage for the Messiah.

prophecies.name: God Will Raise Up David Their King
prophecies.date: Written c. 626–586 BC during the final decades of the southern kingdom of Judah

nt_fulfillments.note: God promises to raise up 'David their king', pointing directly to the Messiah who fulfills the Davidic covenant and rules eternally.

prophecies.name: Rachel Weeping for Her Children
prophecies.date: Written c. 626–586 BC during the final decades of the southern kingdom of Judah

nt_fulfillments.note: Matthew explicitly cites this verse regarding the weeping of captive Israel as typologically fulfilled by the Massacre of the Innocents under King Herod following Christ's birth.

prophecies.name: The New Covenant
prophecies.date: Written c. 626–586 BC during the final decades of the southern kingdom of Judah

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus institutes the New Covenant at the Last Supper, and the author of Hebrews quotes this passage extensively to demonstrate that Christ is the mediator of this new, superior covenant.

prophecies.name: Moses as a Type of the Faithful Son over God's House
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The author of Hebrews cites Numbers 12:7, explicitly stating that Moses' role as a faithful servant in God's house was a typological 'testimony of those things which were to be spoken after' regarding Christ, the Son who is faithful over His own house.

prophecies.name: Wisdom Begotten as the Beginning of His Ways
prophecies.date: Composed mainly c. 950–700 BC during the reigns of Solomon and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Proverbs 8:22-25 in the LXX was a central text in Patristic theology, cited extensively by the Early Church Fathers to defend the pre-existence, eternal generation, and incarnation of the Son of God as the divine Logos.

prophecies.name: Wisdom Present as Co-Creator
prophecies.date: Composed mainly c. 950–700 BC during the reigns of Solomon and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The depiction of Wisdom continually present, rejoicing with the Father, and acting as the master craftsman points directly to the Son's role as the Word through whom all things were made.

prophecies.name: Wisdom's House and the Mingled Feast
prophecies.date: Composed mainly c. 950–700 BC during the reigns of Solomon and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers (such as Augustine and Cyprian) universally interpreted Wisdom building her house as the Word taking on human flesh, and the prepared table with bread and mingled wine as a typological prophecy of the Eucharist.

prophecies.name: A Light to the Gentiles
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Simeon proclaims this fulfillment when holding the infant Jesus, and Paul later explicitly applies it to the spread of the Gospel to the Gentiles.

prophecies.name: Submission to Scourging and Spitting
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

prophecies.name: The Unheard Report Believed by the Nations
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

prophecies.name: Disbelief in the Report of the Servant
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

prophecies.name: Bearing Sickness and Pain
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Matthew explicitly applies this passage to Jesus' physical healing ministry.

prophecies.name: Wounded for Our Sins
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

prophecies.name: Silent as a Lamb Led to the Slaughter
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Quoted explicitly by Philip in Acts to the Ethiopian eunuch as referring to Jesus, and mirrored practically in Christ's silence before his accusers.

prophecies.name: Buried with the Rich
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Fulfilled when Joseph of Arimathea, a rich man, took the body of Jesus and laid it in his own new tomb.

prophecies.name: Numbered Among the Transgressors
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus explicitly quotes this verse to his disciples at the Last Supper in Luke, indicating it must be fulfilled in him.

prophecies.name: The Sure Mercies of David
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Applied by Paul to the resurrection of Jesus, showing the eternal, incorruptible nature of the Messianic covenant.

prophecies.name: The Deliverer from Zion
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

prophecies.name: The Exaltation of the Lord's Christ (Messiah)
prophecies.date: Written c. 960–930 BC after the division of the kingdom, compiling earlier prophetic records

nt_fulfillments.note: Hannah's song contains the first explicit prophetic mention of the Lord's 'Christ' (Anointed One) in Scripture. Zechariah explicitly echoes this in the NT by declaring God has 'raised up a horn of salvation' in the house of David.
Mary's Magnificat directly models the themes and structure of Hannah's song to announce the Incarnation of the Messiah.

prophecies.name: The Raising of a Faithful High Priest
prophecies.date: Written c. 960–930 BC after the division of the kingdom, compiling earlier prophetic records

nt_fulfillments.note: While historically finding near-fulfillment in Zadok replacing the corrupt line of Eli, the Early Church and the Epistle to the Hebrews explicitly typologize this as the definitive raising of Jesus Christ, the merciful and faithful High Priest who perfectly fulfills the will of God eternally.

prophecies.name: The Seed of David and the Everlasting Kingdom
prophecies.date: Written c. 450–425 BC during the post-exilic restoration

nt_fulfillments.note: The Angel Gabriel directly applies this eternal Davidic promise to Jesus at the Annunciation, declaring He will reign over the house of Jacob forever.
In his Pentecost sermon, Peter identifies this prophetic oath regarding David's seed sitting on his throne as fulfilled in the resurrection and exaltation of Jesus Christ.
The author of Hebrews cites the declaration 'I will be to him a father, and he shall be to me a son' to prove the absolute supremacy and divine sonship of Christ.

prophecies.name: The Son of Peace Who Builds God's House
prophecies.date: Written c. 450–425 BC during the post-exilic restoration

nt_fulfillments.note: Solomon's role as the son of peace who builds the physical temple typologically points to Christ, who is the ultimate Prince of Peace raising up the true temple of His body.
Hebrews contrasts the earthly builder with Christ, who fulfills the typology perfectly by being the Son who faithfully presides over God's ultimate house, the Church.

prophecies.name: The Great I AM
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus identifies Himself with the divine name revealed to Moses in the LXX ('I am THE BEING'), asserting His pre-existence and deity.

prophecies.name: Praising God Among the Gentiles
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul explicitly cites this verse to demonstrate that Christ's saving work and ministry include the Gentiles glorifying God.

prophecies.name: Forsaken by God on the Cross
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: Mocked by Spectators
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: Severe Thirst on the Cross
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus' profound thirst on the cross physically fulfills the agonizing dehydration prophetically described in this verse.

prophecies.name: Pierced Hands and Feet
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: This describes the exact method of Roman crucifixion, predicting the piercing of Christ's hands and feet centuries before the practice was invented.

prophecies.name: Casting Lots for His Garments
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Explicitly cited by the Gospel writers as being fulfilled when the Roman soldiers divided Jesus' clothes and cast lots for His seamless tunic.

prophecies.name: Declaring God's Name to the Brethren
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: The author of Hebrews cites this verse to illustrate the resurrected Christ's solidarity with humanity and His establishment of the Church.

prophecies.name: Ascension of the King of Glory
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Widely interpreted by Early Church Fathers (such as Justin Martyr) as a direct typological prophecy of the Ascension, envisioning the gates of heaven opening for the victorious, ascending Christ.

prophecies.name: Committing His Spirit to the Father
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus directly quotes this exact prayer on the cross at the moment of His death to yield His spirit to the Father.

prophecies.name: The Lion of the Tribe of Judah
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The title 'Lion of the tribe of Juda' is explicitly applied to Jesus Christ as the only one worthy to open the scroll.

prophecies.name: The Ruler from Judah and the Expectation of Nations
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The Gospel genealogies establish Jesus' royal descent from Judah. The LXX rendering 'expectation of nations' was heavily cited by Early Church apologists like Justin Martyr to point to Christ's reception by the Gentiles.

prophecies.name: The Foal and Washing Garments in the Blood of the Grape
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers explicitly connected the binding of the foal to Jesus' Triumphal Entry into Jerusalem.
The washing of the robe in the 'blood of the grape' was universally interpreted by the Patristic writers as a direct prophecy of the Eucharist and Christ's blood shed upon the cross.

prophecies.name: The True Bread from Heaven
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus refers to the giving of heavenly bread in the wilderness as a type pointing to Himself as the true, life-giving bread descending from the Father.

prophecies.name: The Eternal Davidic Covenant
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: The angel Gabriel directly applies the promise of the eternal throne of David to Jesus at the Annunciation.

prophecies.name: The Firstborn and Exalted King
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: John identifies Jesus as the 'firstborn from the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth,' mirroring the LXX phrasing here.

prophecies.name: Angelic Protection for the Son
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Quoted during the Temptation in the wilderness, establishing this text as a well-understood type regarding the ultimate divine protection of God's Son.

prophecies.name: The Deliverer Shall Be Called a Nazarene
prophecies.date: Written c. 1050–1000 BC during the early Israeli monarchy

nt_fulfillments.note: Matthew 2:23 states that the prophets foretold Jesus would be called a 'Nazarene.' Because no single Old Testament prophet makes this exact statement, the Early Church and scholars widely recognize this as a typological fulfillment of the Nazarite (or Nazir) vow of Samson, who was consecrated from the womb to be the savior of Israel.

prophecies.name: The Oath to David: The Eternal King from His Descendants
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: The Apostle Peter explicitly cites this psalm in his Pentecost sermon, confirming that God's oath to place the fruit of David's body on his throne was a direct prophecy fulfilled in Jesus Christ.

prophecies.name: The Horn of Salvation and Lamp for the Anointed
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: In the Benedictus, Zechariah directly references this prophetic imagery, declaring that God has raised up a 'horn of salvation' in the house of David.

prophecies.name: Replacement of the Betrayer
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: The Messiah at the Right Hand of God
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus explicitly cites this verse to demonstrate to the Pharisees that the Messiah is greater than David.

prophecies.name: Begotten Before the Morning Star
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: The unique LXX reading 'I have begotten you from the womb before the morning' was universally cited by Early Church Fathers as a direct prophecy of the eternal, pre-incarnate generation of the Son.

prophecies.name: The Eternal Priesthood of Melchizedek
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: The author of Hebrews uses this to establish Jesus' eternal high priesthood overriding the Levitical order.

prophecies.name: The Rejected Stone Becomes the Cornerstone
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: Triumphal Entry and Hosanna
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: Angels Commanded to Worship the Son
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 7th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Hebrews 1:6 explicitly quotes the Septuagint version of Deuteronomy 32:43, applying the command for the angels to worship directly to the Son (Christ) when He is brought into the world.

prophecies.name: Provoking Israel to Jealousy through the Gentiles
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 7th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul cites this verse to explain the Messianic inclusion of the Gentiles, which serves to provoke the unbelieving portion of Israel to jealousy.

prophecies.name: Gentiles Rejoicing with His People
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 7th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul quotes this verse to demonstrate that Christ became a servant to the Jews in order to fulfill the promises, ultimately leading the Gentiles to rejoice and glorify God for His mercy in the Messianic age.

prophecies.name: The Temple Corrupted into a Den of Robbers
prophecies.date: Written c. 626–586 BC during the final decades of the southern kingdom of Judah

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus explicitly quotes this verse to condemn the commercialization of the temple during His ministry, demonstrating the fulfillment of Jeremiah's prophetic warning regarding the corruption of God's house.

prophecies.name: The Innocent Lamb and the Wood of the Cross
prophecies.date: Written c. 626–586 BC during the final decades of the southern kingdom of Judah

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers, such as Justin Martyr and Tertullian, universally cited 'an innocent lamb led to the slaughter' and the phrase 'let us put wood into his bread' (LXX) as a direct typological prophecy of Jesus's passive suffering and crucifixion, interpreting the 'wood' as the cross and the 'bread' as His body.

prophecies.name: The Prophet Like Moses
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 7th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: During Jesus' ministry, the crowds explicitly identified Him as the Prophet foretold by Moses.
Peter and Stephen explicitly cite this exact passage from Deuteronomy, definitively identifying Jesus as the prophesied Prophet.

prophecies.name: Cursed is Everyone Who Hangs on a Tree
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 7th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul explicitly quotes Deuteronomy 21:23 to explain how Christ bore the curse of the law through His crucifixion.
The apostles specifically use the language of Jesus hanging on a 'tree' (xylon in the LXX) rather than a cross, to connect His death directly to this passage.

prophecies.name: The Word Brought Near
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 7th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul explicitly interprets the concepts of ascending to heaven and descending into the deep as typological of the Incarnation (bringing Christ down) and the Resurrection (bringing Christ up from the dead).

prophecies.name: Miracles of the Messiah's Ministry
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus specifically points to these miraculous acts (blind seeing, lame walking, deaf hearing) to answer John the Baptist's disciples regarding His identity as the Messiah.

prophecies.name: The Voice Crying in the Wilderness
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

prophecies.name: The Gentle Servant of the Lord
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The LXX text incorporates 'Jacob' and 'Israel' in verse 1 as interpretive glosses, whereas Matthew's Gospel quotes a version closer to the Hebrew text to apply the Servant identity exclusively and directly to Jesus Christ.

prophecies.name: A Light to the Gentiles
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Simeon explicitly references this prophecy during the presentation of Jesus in the temple, identifying Him as the promised light of revelation for the nations.

prophecies.name: Every Knee Shall Bow to the Lord
prophecies.date: Written c. 740–686 BC during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul applies this exclusive oath of Yahweh directly to Jesus Christ, affirming His divine lordship and exaltation.

prophecies.name: The Messenger of Glad Tidings and Peace
prophecies.date: Written c. 663–612 BC prior to the fall of Nineveh

nt_fulfillments.note: Paul applies this prophetic imagery (parallel to Isaiah 52:7) directly to the apostolic preaching of the Gospel of Christ, signaling the arrival of the Messianic era of peace.

prophecies.name: Rivers of Living Water Flowing from the Temple
prophecies.date: Written c. 593–571 BC during the Babylonian exile

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus identifies Himself as the source of the 'rivers of living water' (the Holy Spirit), invoking the scripture that parallels Ezekiel's vision of life-giving water flowing from the eschatological temple.

prophecies.name: The Righteous Branch of David
prophecies.date: Written c. 626–586 BC during the final decades of the southern kingdom of Judah

nt_fulfillments.note: The angel Gabriel's annunciation to Mary fulfills the promise of a Davidic King who will reign forever.

prophecies.note: In the Septuagint translation of Jeremiah 23:6, the Hebrew phrase 'The LORD our Righteousness' is notably transliterated as 'Josedec' (Iosedek), yet it preserves the overarching direct promise of the Messianic Branch.

prophecies.name: The Bronze Serpent
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus Himself explicitly cites the bronze serpent lifted on a pole to heal the poisoned Israelites as a direct typology of His own crucifixion and the eternal life granted to those who look to Him in faith.

prophecies.name: The Star of Jacob
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Balaam's oracle of a 'star rising out of Jacob' was universally understood by early Christians, such as Justin Martyr and Irenaeus, as a direct prophecy fulfilled by the Star of Bethlehem that led the Magi to the newborn Christ.

prophecies.name: Christ Praised Among the Gentiles
prophecies.date: Written c. 960–930 BC after the division of the kingdom, compiling earlier prophetic records

nt_fulfillments.note: The Apostle Paul explicitly cites this verse (which parallels Psalm 17:50 in the LXX) to demonstrate that Christ's saving work brings the Gentiles to glorify God for His mercy.

prophecies.name: The Messenger Preparing the Way and the Lord Coming to His Temple
prophecies.date: Written c. 440–430 BC during the time of Ezra and Nehemiah

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus explicitly identifies John the Baptist as the messenger of Malachi 3:1 who prepares the way for His own coming.
The sudden coming of the Lord to His temple is fulfilled both in Christ's presentation as an infant and His later authoritative cleansing of the temple.

prophecies.name: The Sun of Righteousness Arising with Healing
prophecies.date: Written c. 440–430 BC during the time of Ezra and Nehemiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The early Church universally understood the 'Sun of Righteousness' as a direct prophetic title for Christ, the light of the world, whose advent is the 'sunrise from on high' spoken of by Zechariah.

prophecies.name: Elijah the Prophet Sent Before the Lord
prophecies.date: Written c. 440–430 BC during the time of Ezra and Nehemiah

nt_fulfillments.note: The angel Gabriel and Jesus Himself identify John the Baptist as the fulfillment of this promised Elijah, sent in the spirit and power of Elijah to turn the hearts of the people before the Messiah's ministry.

prophecies.name: Not a Bone of Him Shall Be Broken
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: Hated Without a Cause
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: A Body Prepared for the Son
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: The Septuagint distinctively translates this as 'a body have you prepared me' (soma de katertisō moi), diverging from the Masoretic reading ('ears you have opened'). The author of Hebrews explicitly relies on this LXX reading to establish the Incarnation as the means of Christ's perfect sacrifice.

prophecies.name: Betrayal by a Close Friend
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: The Divine King's Eternal Throne and Anointing
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: The explicit address of the Davidic king as 'God' (ho theos) whose throne is eternal is directly applied by the author of Hebrews as proof of the Son's divine superiority over the angels.

prophecies.name: The High Priest Enters the Holy Place with Blood
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: Hebrews explicitly identifies the High Priest's entrance into the Holy of Holies on the Day of Atonement as a type of Christ entering the heavenly sanctuary once for all with His own blood.

prophecies.name: The Lord's Passover
prophecies.date: Written c. 1440–1400 BC (traditional Mosaic dating) or compiled c. 6th–5th century BC

nt_fulfillments.note: The Apostle Paul explicitly identifies Christ as 'our Passover lamb' who has been sacrificed.

prophecies.name: The Stone Cut Without Hands and the Eternal Kingdom
prophecies.date: Written c. 536 BC (traditional dating) or c. 165 BC (modern scholarly consensus)

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers, such as Irenaeus and Justin Martyr, universally interpreted the 'stone cut without hands' as a prophecy of Christ's virgin birth (conceived without human seed) and His eternal kingdom which crushes worldly powers.

prophecies.name: The Son of God in the Fiery Furnace
prophecies.date: Written c. 536 BC (traditional dating) or c. 165 BC (modern scholarly consensus)

nt_fulfillments.note: Universally cited by Early Church Fathers (e.g., Irenaeus, Tertullian) as a pre-incarnate appearance (Christophany) of the Word, demonstrating His abiding presence with and deliverance of His persecuted people.

prophecies.name: The Son of Man Coming on the Clouds
prophecies.date: Written c. 536 BC (traditional dating) or c. 165 BC (modern scholarly consensus)

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus explicitly quotes this prophecy during His trial before the Sanhedrin, adopting 'Son of Man' as His primary title and asserting His divine authority and future return.

prophecies.name: The Seventy Weeks and the Suffering Anointed One
prophecies.date: Written c. 536 BC (traditional dating) or c. 165 BC (modern scholarly consensus)

nt_fulfillments.note: This explicit timeline details the arrival of 'Christ the prince', His subsequent death ('the anointed one shall be destroyed' to make atonement), and the ensuing 'abomination of desolation' which Jesus cites directly in His Olivet Discourse.

prophecies.name: The Resurrection of the Dead
prophecies.date: Written c. 536 BC (traditional dating) or c. 165 BC (modern scholarly consensus)

nt_fulfillments.note: Jesus directly echoes the phrasing of Daniel 12:2 when declaring that the dead will hear His voice and come forth to either the resurrection of life or the resurrection of judgment.

prophecies.name: The Nations Rage Against the Christ
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: The early church cited this to explain the conspiracy of Herod, Pontius Pilate, and the Gentiles against Jesus.

prophecies.name: The Begotten Son of God
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Applied by NT authors to Jesus' resurrection and His eternal superiority as the Son.

prophecies.name: The Anointed King Rules the Nations
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: Perfected Praise from Babes
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: Quoted by Jesus during the Triumphal Entry to validate the praise of children in the temple.

prophecies.name: The Son of Man Lowered Then Exalted Over All Things
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

prophecies.name: The Holy One's Flesh Will Not See Corruption
prophecies.date: Composed between c. 1410 BC and c. 430 BC, spanning from the time of Moses and David to the post-exilic period

nt_fulfillments.note: LXX Psalm 15 (Masoretic Psalm 16) is a central apologetic text in Peter and Paul's sermons demonstrating that David explicitly prophesied the bodily resurrection of the Messiah.

prophecies.name: God Will Shake the Heavens and the Earth
prophecies.date: Written c. 520 BC during the rebuilding of the Second Temple

nt_fulfillments.note: The author of Hebrews explicitly quotes Haggai to describe the establishment of Christ's unshakable kingdom and the inauguration of the New Covenant.

prophecies.name: The Greater Glory of the Second Temple and the Giver of Peace
prophecies.date: Written c. 520 BC during the rebuilding of the Second Temple

nt_fulfillments.note: Early Church Fathers universally recognized Christ's physical presence and ministry as the 'greater glory' of the Second Temple, bringing the promised 'peace'. Jesus also identified His own body as the ultimate Temple.
